front: Composer: child prodigy who wrote his first string symphony at 15 and the String Octet at 16; grandson of philosopher Moses Mendelssohn; sibling of composer Fanny Mendelssohn
Composer: conducted the 1829 revival of Bach's St. Matthew Passion at the Singakademie — possible because his grandmother had given him the score as a Christmas gift — starting the Bach Revival
Composer: founded the Leipzig Conservatory and directed the Gewandhaus Orchestra; had an unrequited obsession with soprano Jenny Lind, for whom he wrote 'Hear Ye Israel' in Elijah
Composer: a melody by him became 'Hark! the Herald Angels Sing' (text by Charles Wesley, adapted by William Cummings); his anthem 'Hear My Prayer' includes the solo 'O for the Wings of a Dove'
Suite: overture imitates the braying of Bottom ('hee-hawing' in the strings); the scherzo was inspired by the Walpurgis Night section of Faust; famous Wedding March is used at weddings
back: Incidental Music to A Midsummer Night's Dream (Felix Mendelssohn)
work: Incidental Music to A Midsummer Night's Dream (Op. 61)

front: Suite: opens with whole-note woodwind chords in E major that expand in range; nocturne follows 'what hempen homespuns' with bassoons accompanying the horn melody; Saint-Saëns parodied the scherzo in Carnival of the Animals
back: Incidental Music to A Midsummer Night's Dream (Felix Mendelssohn)
work: Incidental Music to A Midsummer Night's Dream (Op. 61)

front: Concerto: soloist enters in bar 2 (no orchestral tutti); cadenza is fully written out, ending with spiccato arpeggios as the orchestra re-enters; all three movements played attacca; in E minor
back: Violin Concerto in E minor (Felix Mendelssohn)
work: Violin Concerto in E minor, Op. 64

front: Concerto: Joseph Joachim called it 'the heart's jewel'; 1948 recording by Milstein/NY Philharmonic was the first commercial 12-inch LP
back: Violin Concerto in E minor (Felix Mendelssohn)
work: Violin Concerto in E minor, Op. 64

front: Work: E-flat major, written at age 16 for double quartet; scherzo is Allegro leggierissimo, inspired by Faust's Walpurgis Night, and must be played 'pianissimo and staccato'; finale quotes Handel's Hallelujah Chorus
back: String Octet in E-flat, Op. 20 (Felix Mendelssohn)

front: Symphony: nicknamed 'Italian'; first in the standard repertoire to start in major but end in minor; Mendelssohn described it as 'blue sky in A major'; second movement nicknamed 'Pilgrim's March'; finale combines saltarello and tarantella
back: Symphony No. 4 in A major 'Italian' (Felix Mendelssohn)

front: Symphony: nicknamed 'Scottish'; first bars sketched at Holyrood Chapel; scherzo in 2/4 uses the 'Scotch snap' (short-long Lombard rhythm); coda shifts from A minor to A major
back: Symphony No. 3 in A minor 'Scottish' (Felix Mendelssohn)

front: Symphony: nicknamed 'Reformation'; quotes the 'Dresden Amen' in the first movement; opening six-note motif later used by Wagner as the Parsifal Grail theme; finale opens with solo flute playing 'A Mighty Fortress Is Our God'
Oratorio: his final oratorio; premiered at the Birmingham Triennial Festival; Mendelssohn wrote the solo 'Hear Ye Israel' for Jenny Lind

Song Cycle: eight volumes of lyrical piano pieces (6 per book); includes the 'Spring Song' (nicknamed 'Camberwell Green') and the 'Spinner's Song' (nicknamed 'Bee's Wedding'); likely inspired by his sister Fanny
Overture: nicknamed 'Fingal's Cave'; inspired by a trip to Scotland that also produced the Scottish Symphony

Work: Second String Quartet in A minor; uses the song 'Ist es wahr?' as a cyclic motif as homage to Beethoven; the F minor Quartet No. 6 was a 'Requiem' for Fanny, written two months before Felix died
